does: This paper investigates feature death in sparse autoencoders—where learned features never activate during deployment—by identifying activation outliers as the root cause. The authors analyze how dimension-level activation outliers, characterized by large mean magnitude relative to per-token variation, cause features to receive permanently negative pre-activations at initialization based on their alignment with the activation mean. They formalize outlier severity as γ = ‖μ‖/‖σ‖ and demonstrate across 454 model-layer combinations spanning language, vision, protein, and genomic models that this metric strongly predicts initial death rates (Spearman ρ = 0.89 for dead-by-TopK, 0.82 for dead-by-ReLU). The paper shows that while dead features can theoretically revive during training, recovery is prohibitively slow at high γ values because it requires the SAE bias to slowly learn the activation mean.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on sparse autoencoders as mechanistic interpretability tools, following recent efforts to decompose neural network activations into interpretable sparse features. It extends prior observations that SAE configuration and architecture dramatically affect feature death rates by providing a theoretical and empirical explanation for these variations. The paper connects to classical understanding of initialization effects in neural networks, particularly how pre-activation distributions affect feature learning dynamics. It also relates to broader work on superposition in neural networks and feature learning efficiency, addressing why some models suffer severe capacity waste through dead features while others do not. The investigation incorporates insights from statistics and signal processing about activation distributions across diverse model architectures.
enables: This work points toward practical improvements in sparse autoencoder design through mean-centering or similar pre-processing techniques that reduce outlier severity. The identified relationship between γ and feature death enables principled hyperparameter selection and model-specific SAE configuration strategies, potentially allowing practitioners to anticipate and mitigate feature death before training. The formalization of outlier severity as a predictive metric could guide future research into other initialization schemes and training dynamics that interact with activation statistics. Understanding these mechanisms opens possibilities for designing SAEs that maintain consistent performance across diverse architectures, and suggests paths toward better mechanistic interpretability tools for increasingly varied model families including vision transformers, protein structures, and other emerging architectures.
